1.2 Project Overview, Brief History and Justification
Delhi Panipat Ambala is 198 KMs long Section. This section is electrified and is most popular amongst
the  rail  users  being  the  shortest  route  which  connects  Northern  parts  of  the  country  with  Eastern,
Western and Southern parts through Rail network. Due to these reasons, this section is very important
and is one of the most heavily worked sections of Northern Railway. The sub section Delhi Panipat
falls  under  National  Capital Region.  In  view  of  Central  Government's  Policy  to  boost infrastructure
across the National Capital Region, the citizens have now shifted to different small towns like Badli,
Narela, Sonipat, Gannaur, Samalkha, Panipat etc. These people daily travel by EMU services and other
trains  to  Delhi  and  return  to  their  homes  in  the  evenings.  At  present  10  pairs  are  running  suburban
services. The commuters are demanding running of more suburban trains on this section but due to line
capacity constraints, the demands have not yet been fulfilled. In addition, long distance Mail/Express
trains pass through this section for important cities like Ambala Cantt., Ludhiana, Jallandhar, Amritsar,
Patiala, Chandigarh, Kalka, Shimla, Pathankot, Jammu etc. Udhampur-Srinagar-Baramula rail link will
be  commissioned  shortly  which  will  further  enhance  traffic  over  Delhi-Ambala  section.  In  view  of
increased demand for running more trains, on an average, 3-4 more trains are being added every year.

32.Final Location Survey for 3rd & 4th Line: Ambala (193.60Km)
Delhi Panipat Ambala is 198 KMs long Section. This section is electrified and is most popular amongst
the  rail  users  being  the  shortest  route  which  connects  Northern  parts  of  the  country  with  Eastern,
Western and Southern parts through Rail network. Due to these reasons, this section is very important
and is one of the most heavily worked sections of Northern Railway. The sub section Delhi Panipat
falls  under  National  Capital Region.  In  view  of  Central  Government's  Policy  to  boost infrastructure
across the National Capital Region, the citizens have now shifted to different small towns like Badli,
Narela, Sonipat, Gannaur, Samalkha, Panipat etc. These people daily travel by EMU services and other
trains  to  Delhi  and  return  to  their  homes  in  the  evenings.  At  present  10  pairs  are  running  suburban
services. The commuters are demanding running of more suburban trains on this section but due to line
capacity constraints, the demands have not yet been fulfilled. In addition, long distance Mail/Express
trains pass through this section for important cities like Ambala Cantt., Ludhiana, Jallandhar, Amritsar,
Patiala, Chandigarh, Kalka, Shimla, Pathankot, Jammu etc. Udhampur-Srinagar-Baramula rail link will
be  commissioned  shortly  which  will  further  enhance  traffic  over  Delhi-Ambala  section.  In  view  of
increased demand for more trains, on an average, 3-4 more trains are being added every year. In addition
to this, a 3rd & 4th Line Jind-Sonipat has also commissioned and new PFT at Mohana, Bhambhewa are
being set up on this section. New silo siding at Naultha on Rohtak-Panipat section is also going to be
commissioned. More industries are being set up over Northern region e.g., augmentation of generation
capacity of Tau Devi Lal Thermal Power Plant at Panipat, setting of extra plant by M/s. 10C at Baholi.
Due to rapid industrialization of this area, the freight traffic has increased considerably and is likely to
grow further. PFT at Diwana, Bodwalmajri and Rajlugarhi are already functional on this section. Rall
Vikas Navinikaran Karkhana at Rajlugarhi is recently established. New goods shed at Dhirpur is being
developed on PPP model by HTPL. In view of the saturated capacity, introduction of any new train is
likely to adversely impact the mobility and speed of goods trains on the section. Moreover, the growing
demand for introduction of Vande Bharat/Shatabdi trains over DUK Section would restrict the path of
at least four freight trains. With the anticipated growth of the Indian economy, traffic is expected to
further grow 5-6% per annum. Therefore, adding the 3rd & 4th line corridor in this section would not
only manage the projected growth in traffic but also allow seamless mobility and operational flexibility
in train operations, including during accidents.

The history of the rail line between Delhi and Ambala dates back to the early years of the British colonial
period in India. The Delhi to Ambala section is part of the larger rail network in Northern India, which
played  a  crucial  role  in  connecting  various  regions  for  trade,  military  movements,  and  general
transportation. Here’s a brief overview of its development:

53.played a crucial role in connecting: transportation. Here’s a brief overview of its development:

53.col_2: The first railway line in India was inaugurated in 1853 between Mumbai (then Bombay) and
Thane. Following this, the British began expanding the railway network to connect major cities,
including those in the northern region.
The Delhi-Ambala section was part of the Northern Railway zone. The line connected the two
important cities in North India, linking the region with the larger network connecting Delhi to
various parts of the country.
The rail line was officially opened in 1869, and it was built to improve transportation of goods,
especially agricultural produce, and to facilitate the movement of troops during the British Raj.
The Delhi to Ambala rail line had great strategic importance, particularly during the British
colonial era. Ambala was an important military station and depot, so connecting it with Delhi
made troop movement easier.
It was also part of a larger plan to expand the railway network in the north to improve trade and
movement of resources like coal and agricultural produce from Punjab and Haryana to Delhi
and beyond.
Like many other major railway lines in India, the Delhi-Ambala line underwent electrification.
This  was  completed  in  phases,  with  the  electrification  of  the  Delhi-Ambala  route  being
completed in 1996, which was part of a larger effort to modernize the rail infrastructure.
The line today serves as a critical link for passenger trains as well as freight. The city of Ambala
is a major junction where several important routes converge, and the railway station is one of
the busiest in the region.

54.•: In conclusion, this railway line is one of the vital arteries for northern India’s rail network, serving the
transportation needs of millions of passengers and tons of goods annually.
